title: Where Power Remembers Its Shifts

features.name: power-chip
features.desc-on-touch: A massive black IC, pins down, at the board's heart. The pins hum at subsonic pitch—the rhythm of shift-work, the rota from 1981 onwards. Each pin carries one technician's route. The archive recorded their footsteps, but it hears them as current.

features.name: trace-memory
features.desc-on-touch: Gold traces run the board's length, each one a circuit completed by hand-soldering: 1952, 1967, 1978, 1993, 2001. The dates are burned into the copper. If you follow one with your fingertip, you can feel the worker who made it—the hesitation, the skill, the weariness at hour four.

features.name: phantom-load
features.desc-on-touch: A capacitor the size of a thermos, still holding charge it received in 1989. The circuit around it has been dead for seventeen years. It will never discharge. It holds the memory of being needed.

features.name: shift-notation
features.desc-on-touch: Pencil marks in the margin of the schematic—worker names, times, voltage readings, one person's complaint: hums too loud. Another reply, underlined: it's supposed to. The notation is not power data. It is a conversation frozen in margins.

inscription: the machines shut down but they never forgot their shifts

file.name: Shift_Roster_PowerRoom_1981-2006.csv
file.note: Technician names, hours logged; appears to correlate exactly with building's power draw curve; correlation suggests workers are data, data is workers

exits.label: back to the forgotten room where the last shift ended
toward the buried substations where all the town's power remembers itself
into deeper strata where machines still dream of being needed
